question: The CMB blackbody spectrum: characteristics and significance.

options: Almost perfect blackbody at $T_{\rm CMB} = 2.7255(6)$ K; deviation from Planck spectrum $|\Delta T/T| < 10^{-5}$ (FIRAS COBE 1996); confirms thermal equilibrium in early universe; spectrum frozen at decoupling ($z \sim 1100$); cooled by cosmic expansion from $\sim 3000$ K then to 2.7 K now
CMB is non-thermal, dominated by stellar emission (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (a common misconception)
CMB temperature is 1 K and rising (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (not supported by the evidence) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Spectrum dominated by single emission line (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Discovery: Penzias-Wilson (1964) — Nobel 1978; Bell Labs horn antenna detected isotropic 3 K signal. COBE FIRAS (Mather, 1990-92): measured spectrum to 50 ppm — perfectly Planck. Mean $T = 2.7255 \pm 0.0006$ K. Photon number density $n_\gamma = 411$ cm$^{-3}$; energy density $u_\gamma = aT^4 \approx 0.26$ eV/cm³. Decoupling: at $T \sim 3000$ K, free electrons recombined with protons → universe transparent. Photons last-scattered at $z_* \approx 1090$, came from spherical 'surface of last scattering' at comoving distance $\sim 14$ Gpc. Cooling: $T \propto 1/a$ as photons redshift, $T(z) = T_0(1+z)$. Direct verification at $z = 2.3$ via CO absorption in DLA: $T = 9.15 \pm 0.7$ K, matching prediction.

active.prompt: Match each CMB experiment with its main result:

pairs.definition: Discovered CMB anisotropies $\Delta T/T \sim 10^{-5}$; confirmed blackbody spectrum; Nobel 2006 (Mather, Smoot)
Mapped full sky at ~degree resolution; determined $\Omega_b, \Omega_c, H_0$ to few percent; began precision cosmology era
Most precise full-sky CMB to date; arcminute resolution; established $\Lambda$CDM parameters to permille
Sub-arcminute resolution; tested damping tail, lensing, polarisation; complement to space missions

active.explanation: CMB power spectrum $C_\ell^{TT}$ peaks: first at $\ell \sim 200$ (1° angular scale) — acoustic horizon at recombination; higher peaks: $\ell \sim 540$ (2nd), $\ell \sim 810$ (3rd), etc. Damping tail beyond $\ell \sim 1000$. Polarisation (E-mode) peaks shifted, plus B-mode from lensing + (potentially) primordial GW.

question: Acoustic peaks in CMB power spectrum: what physics produces them?

options: Pre-recombination, baryon-photon plasma oscillates: photons push, baryons pull, sound waves at $c_s = c/\sqrt 3$. At recombination, oscillations frozen at amplitudes depending on phase when photons decouple. Modes that just completed half-oscillation (compression) → 1st peak at $\ell \sim 200$. Compression peaks at odd multiples of fundamental; rarefactions at even. Diffusion damping (Silk) suppresses small scales

Photons in CMB scatter off stars producing peaks (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this describes a different phenomenon)

Peaks come from supernova afterglow (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (not supported by the evidence) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)

Peaks are detector artefacts (this conflates cause and effect) (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: Tightly-coupled baryon-photon fluid before $z \sim 1100$. Sound waves driven by gravitational potentials (set by dark matter). Sound horizon at recombination $r_s \approx 150$ Mpc (comoving). Acoustic angular scale $\theta_* = r_s/D_A \approx 1°$, $\ell \sim \pi/\theta_* \sim 200$. Peak positions probe geometry (angular-diameter distance $D_A$, hence $\Omega_k, H_0$). Peak amplitudes probe matter/baryon content: baryons increase odd peaks (compression boost), decrease even (rarefaction). Higher peaks: dark matter increases, baryon dampens via $\rho_b/\rho_\gamma$ — set by $\Omega_b h^2$. Beyond $\ell \sim 1000$: Silk damping (photon diffusion smears small-scale modes), exponential suppression. Lensing redistributes power at high $\ell$, measurable signature.

question: Sachs-Wolfe effect: how does the CMB get its large-scale anisotropy?

options: Photons climbing out of gravitational potential wells at recombination experience redshift: $\Delta T/T = \Phi/3c^2$ (Sachs-Wolfe). Integrated Sachs-Wolfe (ISW): time-varying $\Phi$ along line of sight adds extra contribution; in $\Lambda$-dominated era, $\Phi$ decays → contributes to large-scale CMB-LSS cross-correlation, dark-energy probe
Large-scale CMB anisotropy comes purely from instrumental noise (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)

Sachs-Wolfe predicts $\Delta T/T = 1$ — large fluctuations (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
No effect on CMB from gravitational potentials (this describes a different phenomenon) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: Sachs-Wolfe (1967): photon temperature shift at last-scattering surface includes (1) intrinsic density of plasma $\delta T/T \sim -\Phi/3c^2$ (cold spots = potential wells where photons start) + (2) gravitational redshift climbing out $+\Phi/c^2$ + (3) Doppler from peculiar velocity. Net for adiabatic perturbations on large scales: $\Delta T/T = \Phi/3c^2$ — cold spots in CMB = matter overdensities. Late-time ISW: $\Phi$ evolves when not in matter era; in $\Lambda$ era, $\Phi$ decays as universe expands → photons crossing wells get net energy boost → late-ISW anisotropy at large angular scales. Detection: cross-correlate CMB with low-$z$ LSS (galaxies, ISW signal $\sim$ 1\% effect). Confirms dark energy / accelerated expansion. Rees-Sciama: nonlinear analogue.

question: CMB polarisation: how does Thomson scattering generate E and B modes?

options: Thomson scattering of unpolarised CMB photons by free electrons at recombination produces linear polarisation if local quadrupole anisotropy present. Polarisation decomposed into curl-free E-modes (sourced by density/scalar perturbations) and divergence-free B-modes (sourced by tensor/GW perturbations, or by gravitational lensing of E to B)

CMB is completely unpolarised (this describes a different phenomenon) (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Only B-mode polarisation exists; E-mode forbidden (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Polarisation from circular not linear modes (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this describes a different phenomenon) (a common misconception)
passive.explanation: Quadrupole anisotropy at electron's location → polarised Thomson scattering. Quadrupole amplitude $\sim 10\%$ of temperature anisotropy → polarisation $\sim 10\%$ of $\Delta T/T$, hence $\Delta T_{\rm pol} \sim$ few μK. E/B decomposition (Kamionkowski-Kosowsky-Stebbins, Zaldarriaga-Seljak 1997): like electric/magnetic vector fields. Scalar perturbations: only E-modes (curl-free pattern around hot/cold spots). Tensor (GW): both E and B. Gravitational lensing: converts E → B via deflection. Measured: E-modes at Planck/ACT/SPT precision; lensing B-modes detected (Planck 2015, ACT, SPT, POLARBEAR); primordial B-modes still hidden by dust + lensing — BICEP/Keck and future LiteBIRD, CMB-S4 push toward $r \sim 10^{-3}$.

question: CMB lensing: how is it measured and what does it reveal?

options: Large-scale structure between us and recombination gravitationally deflects CMB photons by $\sim 2'$; remaps temperature/polarisation patterns. Detect via 4-point statistics (lensing-induced non-Gaussianity); reconstructs projected mass map up to $z \sim 1100$. Measured by Planck, ACT, SPT; probes total matter (incl. dark matter, neutrinos), late-time evolution, $\sigma_8$

CMB doesn't gravitationally lens (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Lensing only affects optical light, not microwave (this describes a different phenomenon) (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence)

Lensing of CMB is too weak to measure (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation:
Lensing potential $\phi(\hat n) = -2\int_0^{z_*} (D_{Ls}/D_L D_s)\Phi d\chi$; deflection angle $\alpha = 
abla\phi$, RMS $\sim 2'$. Lensing remaps observed temperature: $T(\hat n) = T_{m unlensed}(\hat n + \alpha)$, smooths acoustic peaks at high $\ell$. Reconstruct $\phi$ via quadratic estimator $\hat\phi \sim TT, TE, EB$ correlations beyond Gaussian. Planck 2018: lensing power $A_\phi = 1.06 \pm 0.04$ — slight excess vs $\Lambda$CDM ($A_{m lens}$ tension at $\sim 2\sigma$). ACT, SPT, Planck: $S/N \sim 40-60$ on $A_\phi$. Information: (i) integrated dark matter from $z \sim 0 $ to 1100; (ii) constrain $\sum m_
u < 0.12$ eV combined with BAO; (iii) cross-correlation with galaxy surveys → growth of structure. Future: CMB-S4 will measure lensing to $\sim 1\%$, deliver $\sum m_
u$ at $> 5\sigma$.

question: Spectral distortions of CMB: $\mu$ and $y$ types, their physics and detection.

options: Energy injection $z > 2\times 10^6$: Compton thermalises but Bremsstrahlung can't restore photon number → chemical potential $\mu$-distortion (Bose-Einstein form). At $5\times 10^4 < z < 2\times 10^6$: only Compton (Kompaneets), gives $y$-distortion (depleted at low $\nu$, enhanced at high). Probe primordial spectrum, decaying DM, primordial BHs, Silk damping. FIRAS limits $|\mu| < 9\times 10^{-5}$, $|y| < 1.5\times 10^{-5}$; PIXIE proposed to reach $10^{-8}$

CMB spectrum exactly blackbody; no distortions possible (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception)

Distortions purely from instrument noise (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (true only as a special case, not in general) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence)

Only thermal SZ effect contributes (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Pre-FIRAS expected CMB to deviate strongly from BB if exotic early universe processes occurred. FIRAS measured $\mu < 9\times 10^{-5}$, $y < 1.5\times 10^{-5}$ — confirmed exquisite thermal equilibrium. Physics of distortion types: photons created by Bremsstrahlung at low frequencies; can be thermalised by Compton scattering only above kinetic equilibrium temperature. Sources of energy injection: (i) Silk damping of acoustic waves at $k \sim 50$ Mpc$^{-1}$ — predicted $\mu \sim 10^{-8}$ from $\Lambda$CDM; (ii) recombination lines from H/He at $z \sim 1000$; (iii) decaying or annihilating dark matter; (iv) primordial BH evaporation (constraints on $\sim 10^{13}-10^{14}$ g PBHs); (v) cosmic-ray heating. Beyond FIRAS: PIXIE (US, NASA SmEx proposal), PRISM (ESA) — target $\sigma_\mu \sim 10^{-8}$, would detect $\Lambda$CDM Silk distortion.
